coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots piecing together into graphene on nano Au for overall water splitting
coredata.dc:creator: Liang, Junwei
Liu, Yuxiang
Si, Zhichun
Wei, Guodan
Weng, Duan
Kang, Feiyu
coredata.dc:description: Utilizing photocatalysts to split water into H2 and H2O2 simultaneously is an ideal but challenging process to obtain clean H2 energy and high valuable H2O2. Most of the photocatalysts currently studied for the overall water splitting are converting water into H2 and O2, resulting in further need of gas separation. Here, we reported a photocatalyst which can utilize the nano-Au based localized surface plasmonic resonance (LSPR) effect to produce H2 and H2O2 simultaneously. Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) pieced together into large graphene through a catalytic process by chloroauric acid, in which dodecahedron nano-Au particles can also be obtained concurrently. Then Au wrapped up by graphene can be in situ formed with good interfaces. Photocatalytic activity tests revealed that the as prepared graphene@Au catalysts show efficient photocatalytic overall water splitting activity with a H2 evolution rate of 65.6 μmol g−1 h−1 and H2O2 generation rate of 49.7 μmol g−1 h−1. This work provides a feasible route for the facile fabrication of graphene through the bottom-up polycondensation of N-GQDs by amide bonds and new insights into the synthesis of photocatalysts for overall water splitting into H2 and H2O2.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Graphene
Amide bond
LSPR
Overall water splitting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The design, preparation and mechanism of graphene@Au

Moreover, we would also thank the financial support from Basic Research Project of Shenzhen , China (Grant No. JCYJ20160301150948119 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the synthesis of graphene@Au photocatalyst;
(b) FT-IR spectra of N-GQDs and graphene@Au; (c) Raman spectra of graphene@Au. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) TEM image of N-GQDs (inset: HRTEM image); (b) TEM image of graphene@Au; (c) AFM height profile of graphene@Au; (d) HRTEM image of graphene@Au. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Full XPS spectrum of N-GQDs and graphene@Au; high resolution XPS spectra of (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s; (d) high resolution XPS spectra of Au 4f for graphene@Au. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) UV–vis absorption of N-GQDs and graphene@Au; (b) Mott-Schottky plots, (c) UPS spectrum and (d) band structure diagram of graphene@Au. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) PL spectra and (b) transient photocurrent response of N-GQDs and graphene@Au; (c) Product evolution of graphene@Au; (d) Product evolution rates of N-GQDs, graphene@Au under visible light, bare Au, graphene and graphene@Au. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic illustration of the overall water splitting mechanism of graphene@Au under light irradiation. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Summary of photocatalysts for photocatalytic H2 and H2O2 production.

row.entry: living Chlorella vulgaris and needle coke commodified g-C3N4
Pt loaded K+ doped graphitic carbon nitride
Pt loaded Na+ doped graphitic carbon nitride

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots piecing together into graphene on nano Au for overall water splitting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shenzhen International Graduate School, Tsinghua University, Shenzhen, 518055, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shenzhen International Graduate School, Tsinghua University, Shenzhen, 518055, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Tsinghua University, Beijing, 100086, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Tsinghua University, Beijing, 100086, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Utilizing photocatalysts to split water into H2 and H2O2 simultaneously is an ideal but challenging process to obtain clean H2 energy and high valuable H2O2. Most of the photocatalysts currently studied for the overall water splitting are converting water into H2 and O2, resulting in further need of gas separation. Here, we reported a photocatalyst which can utilize the nano-Au based localized surface plasmonic resonance (LSPR) effect to produce H2 and H2O2 simultaneously. Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) pieced together into large graphene through a catalytic process by chloroauric acid, in which dodecahedron nano-Au particles can also be obtained concurrently. Then Au wrapped up by graphene can be in situ formed with good interfaces. Photocatalytic activity tests revealed that the as prepared graphene@Au catalysts show efficient photocatalytic overall water splitting activity with a H2 evolution rate of 65.6 μmol g−1 h−1 and H2O2 generation rate of 49.7 μmol g−1 h−1. This work provides a feasible route for the facile fabrication of graphene through the bottom-up polycondensation of N-GQDs by amide bonds and new insights into the synthesis of photocatalysts for overall water splitting into H2 and H2O2.

Schematic illustration of the overall water splitting mechanism of graphene@Au under light irradiation.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen is considered as one of the best candidates for storing solar energy meeting the growing clean energy demand [1–3]. Photocatalytic water splitting is one of the promising means for hydrogen production, since Fujishima and Honda first discovered that TiO2 could photocatalyze hydrogen evolution reaction in 1972 [4]. There are two completely different mechanisms for photocatalytic water splitting: one is a two-electron process (2H2O → H2 + H2O2) and the other is a four-electron process (2H2O → 2H2 + O2) [5–7]. Kang et al. reported that the two-electron process involving H2 and H2O2 as final products could be achieved on carbon dots [8] and graphene based catalysts [9]. However, it is still very challenging to prepare such kind of active photocatalysts.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is an ideal photocatalyst for hydrogen evolution because of low cost, high stability, nontoxic, suitable and adjustable bandgap, oxygen-rich surface and high surface area [10–18]. In addition, GQDs exhibit relatively good electron transfer abilities which would be beneficial to improve the separation of photo-generated charges [8,19–21]. The composition of GQDs with other catalysts may further improve the photocatalytic performance by promoting the electron transfer via building heterojunctions. Previous studies revealed that GQDs could serve as active sites for water dissociative and adsorption because of its oxygen-rich surface [8,22]. Apart from the above, the functional groups on surface of GQDs, such as –COOH and –NH2, can strongly interact with metal catalysts, facilitating the dispersion of metal species [23–29].

However, GQDs have poor response in the visible light region, which hinders its application in photocatalysis. Metal nanoparticles (NPs) with localized surface plasmonic resonance effect (LSPR), such as gold (Au) and silver (Ag), may provide a solution to enhance light absorption of GQDs. LSPR is the resonant photon-induced coherent oscillation of charge at the metal–dielectric interface. In the small particle limit, a resonantly enhanced field builds up inside the particle can produce a dipolar field outside the particle, leading to improved electron concentration in the immediate vicinity of the particle surface [30–35]. The Au nanoflowers wrapped with a thin layer of IrO2 could significantly reduce the overpotential of OER from 326 mV to 286 mV at 10 mA cm−2 under light [36]. Similarly, Mondal et al. also reported that the Au nanorods (NRs) with Pt on the two ends can reach a photocurrent of 10 mA cm−2 with an overpotential of 140 mV under light irradiation, which was more than twofold of the current density in the dark [37]. If the GQDs and LSPR metal can interact closely, the hot electrons of LSPR metal may also transfer into the conduction band of GQDs and involve in the surface reactions. Therefore, it is reasonable to utilize the LSPR hot electrons to enhance the light absorption of GQDs for water splitting.

The graphene can be prepared via both “top-down” (such as Hummer’s method) and “bottom-up” method (such as CVD). And the application of graphene in photocatalysis is very common to promote the charge transfer between the semiconductor catalysts and metal sites or other components [38–46]. Unfortunately, the composition of metal and a given graphene is difficult due to their distinct surface properties. It is very promising to prepare graphene coated metal nano particles via in situ formation of graphene on surface of metal nanoparticles. However, the preparation of graphene always needs high temperature [47], which may lead to the instability of metal nanoparticles. Recently, the preparation of graphene via the jointing of carbon domains with carboxyl and amino groups are realized at 200 °C [48]. In this work, nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were pieced together into graphene through a chloroauric acid catalytic process in which dodecahedron nano-Au particles can also be obtained concurrently. Then the graphene coated Au nanoparticles can be obtained in one step in solution at low temperature.

Herein, a method of preparing graphene coating Au nanoparticle composite with in situ formed graphene@Au interfaces was presented. The fabrication of graphene was proved via the bottom-up polycondensation of N-GQDs by amide bonds. The graphene facilitate the transition of hot electrons from Au nanoparticles to graphene, triggering 100% selectivity of overall water splitting to H2 and H2O2. These findings also provide new insights into the bottom-up synthesis of graphene and high efficient LSPR photocatalysts.

Chloroauric acid tetrahydrate (HAuCl4•4H2O, 48–50%) was bought from Macklin. Glucose (GC, ≥99.5%), Ammonia solution (AR, 25–28%) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai). Nafion D520 dispersion was purchased from Alfa Aesar. All the chemicals were used as received without further purification, and all solutions were prepared with ultra-pure water having a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ.

The N-GQDs were prepared by a typical hydrothermal method. Briefly, 20 g glucose was heated at 180 °C under stirring for 30 min in air and then cooled to room temperature. After dissolving, dialyzing and freeze-drying, the light yellow powder was obtained. Then 360 mg powder was dispersed in 60 mL aqueous ammonia under stirred to get an absolutely homogeneous solution. The suspension was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 10 h. After natural cooling to room temperature, the resulting solution was dialyzed for 24 h and dried at 80 °C. The dried product was then dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 12 h. Finally, the pure N-GQDs was obtained.

Typically, 100 mg N-GQDs was dissolved in 30 mL ultra-pure water under stirring to get an absolutely homogeneous solution. Then 2 mL chloroauric acid solution (10 mM) were added into the above solution with stirring. After 30 min, the suspension was transferred to a capped glass bottle and heated at 50 °C for 4 h. Subsequently, the cooled solution was dialyzed with a 1000D dialysis bag for 48 h and then dried at 80 °C.

The microstructure and morphology were observed using field emission transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F30) equipped with a high-angle annular dark field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) accessory. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) was examined on a MFP3D microscopy. Chemical states and composition of catalysts were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS, PHI-5000 Versaprobe, II) with monochromatic Al Kα radiation. The Raman spectra were measured by a HORIBA LabRAM HR800 Raman microscope with HeNe laser of 532 nm. And the photoluminescence (PL) spectra was collected on a laser Raman spectrometer (HORIBA LabRAM HR800) at 325 nm excitation wavelength. The UV–vis absorption spectra was obtained on SHIMADZU UV-2600 UV–vis spectrophotometer. The ultraviolet photoelectron spectrometer (UPS) tests were carried on the X-ray photoelectron spectra (Thermo Fisher, ESCALAB 250i) and the Mott–Schottky (MS) tests were carried on the electrochemical workstation (Chenhua Shanghai, CHI660E).

Photocatalytic reactions were carried out in a gas-closed circulation photocatalytic system with a vacuum line (Ceaulight, CELSPH2N-D9). In detail, 20 mg catalysts were suspended in 100 mL ultra-pure water that was cooled at 8 °C with continuous stirring and vacuumed for 30 min to remove residual air before evaluation. A 300 W Xe lamp was applied as the light source. The hydrogen gas was measured through a gas chromatography (Ceaulight, GC-7920) equipped with a thermal conductivity detector (TCD), and high purity helium is used as carrier gas. The concentration of H2O2 in the photocatalytic reaction system was determined by UV–vis spectroscopy using N,N-diethyl-p-phenylenediamine sulfate (DPD, Sigma) as the peroxide indicator. Typically, 1 mL reaction supernatant and 1 mL phosphate buffer (pH = 6) were added into the cuvette, and then 0.05 mL DPD (30 mM) and 0.05 mL Horse Radish Peroxidase (HRP, Sigma, 30U/mL) were also added to the cuvette, which caused the colour change to pink. The concentration of H2O2 was then detected by measuring the absorbance of the pink product at a characteristic peak wavelength of 551 nm.

The Photoelectrochemical experiments were carried out in a three electrodes system, using a platinum plate as the counter electrode and a Ag/AgCl (3.5 M KCl) electrode as the reference electrode. The photocatalyst powder was coated onto the FTO (fluoride tin oxide) glass to serve as a working electrode. Typically, 10 mg of the sample was dispersed in 400 mL isopropyl containing 20 mL of 5% Nafion solution under ultrasonication for 30 min. Then 40 μL of well dispersed slurry was coated onto one 1 × 1.5 cm2 FTO glass, and the coated area was kept as 1 cm2. 100 mL of 0.1 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution was used for electrolyte. A 300 W Xe lamp was used as light source, and the incident light intensity was controlled at 20 mA/cm2 on average. A bias voltage of 0.2 V vs. Ag/AgCl and 20 s on/off period was adopted for the transient photocurrent measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The design, preparation and mechanism of graphene@Au
ce:section.ce:para: The graphene@Au photocatalysts were prepared through a facile strategy shown in Fig. 1 (a). The preparation of GQDs with oxygenate-rich surface was carried out via a heat treatment method using glucose as the starting materials [49]. N-GQDs were obtained arising from GQDs by an ammonium assisted hydrothermal method at 160 °C for 10 h. The obtained N-GQDs were re-dispersed in a solution containing certain amount of chloroauric acid and then heated at 50 °C for a redox and polycondensation process. If GQDs solution was mixed with chloroauric acid and treated in a same process, the solution was still clear and no graphene and Au nanoparticles can be observed. So, it is reasonable to claim that hydrochloric acid catalyzed the graphitization. Amide bond formation was considered as the key important reaction in polycondensation of N-GQDs, which was consolidated by FTIR and Raman results.

FT-IR spectra show the changes of the functional groups on sample surfaces, as shown in Fig. 1 (b). The peaks at 1011 cm−1, 1414 cm−1, 1767 cm−1 are assigned to C–O, OH and CO of carbonyl groups on N-GQDs. The peak of CO on graphene@Au shifted to 1704 cm−1, indicating the removal of hydroxyl in carboxyl and the formation of amide bond [47]. The presence of C–N (1390 cm−1) strongly demonstrates the formation of amide on graphene@Au [50]. Au3+ ions could be reduced by surface aldehyde/ketone/hydroxyl aldehyde-like groups on N-GQDs and then more terminal –OH species would generate on N-GQDs as indicated by the presence of peaks at 3400 cm−1 over graphene@Au.

The Raman spectroscopy shown in Fig. 1 (c) suggests the formation of graphene structure. The characteristic D and G bands of graphene@Au present at 1357 and 1575 cm−1, and the 2D peak locates at 2816 cm−1, indicating plenty of defects on in situ formed graphene. The high ratio of D-band (ID) to G-band (IG) (ID/IG = 0.79) also reveals the low graphitization degree of graphene@Au. The defects on graphene may facilitate the dissociative adsorption of H2O molecules on catalyst. The broad 2D peak at 2816 cm−1 also verified the formation of multilayer graphene rather than single-layer graphene in graphene@Au [51].

From the results above, it is reasonably to claim a mechanism of preparation process: Au3+ ions first coordinately adsorbed on surface nitrogen groups of one N-GQD and then reduced to metal atoms by surface aldehyde/ketone/hydroxyl aldehyde-like groups [52]; then, the increasing metal atoms aggregate into Au nanoparticles which could coordinate more nitrogen groups on different N-GQDs; so the distance between N-GQDs were greatly reduced and thereby the polycondensation reactions via amid formation could be triggered more easily and then the bigger nano-graphene pieces generated. Because the bond strengths of Au-N x in amid groups on graphene (the reduction capability of a peptide depends on the presence of certain reducing amino acid residues [53]) are weaker than those on N-GQDs, more N-GQDs adsorbed on nano-gold and pieced together into nano-graphene. Then, nano-graphene grew into large graphene coating on Au tightly. Hence Au coated by graphene can be in situ formed, benefiting the formation of good interfaces between Au and graphene, which can be verified by TEM results.

From Fig. 2 (a), the self-made N-GQDs are uniform nanoparticles in an average size of 5 nm. From Fig. 2 (b), lamellar carbon and Au NPs composite can be clearly seen. As shown in Fig. 2 (d), the HRTEM image of graphene@Au shows that the dodecahedron nano-Au particle was wrapped up by few layers graphene (6–10 layers) and the particle size of Au is about 20 nm. GQDs on graphene surface can also be found. Interestingly, the crystal surface of in situ formed graphene is parallel to the surface of the gold nanoparticles. From the atomic force microscopy (AFM) in Fig. 2 (c), the thickness of graphene was 3.6 nm (about 10 layers), consisting with the results in Fig. 2 (d).

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to determine the composition and surface chemical state of elementals on samples. The full XPS spectrum in Fig. 3 (a) confirms the presence of C, N, O and Au elements on catalysts. Fig. 3 (b) shows the high resolution XPS spectra of C 1s which are calibrated with C 1s at 284.8 eV. As can be seen, both of them present a sharp peak at 284.8 eV corresponding to the C–C bonds. Other peaks of N-GQDs correspond to various C–O bonding configurations could also be found, including sp3 carbon of C–O/C–N at 286.2 eV and carbonyls of CO at 288.0 eV [54]. However, new peaks at 288.6 eV correspond to CO of amide bond, and peaks at 286.4 eV correspond to C–N present on graphene@Au [55]. The high resolution XPS spectra of N 1s (Fig. 3 (c)) also confirm the formation of amide bond. The N 1s bands of graphene@Au shows a shift from 399.9 eV (-NH2) on N-GQDs, to 399.5 eV (N–H) by 0.4 eV [56]. All of above results verify that the N-GQDs could piece up into graphene connected by amide bonds through a bottom-up growth under the activation of chloroauric acid. Fig. 3 (d) shows the high resolution XPS spectra of graphene@Au, the peaks at 83.9 and 87.6 eV are attributed to Au 4f7/2 and Au 4f5/2 of metallic Au.

Fig. 4 (a) shows the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of samples. N-GQDs have strong absorption at 255 nm refers to sp2 hybridized structure resulted by C–C and CC. Graphene@Au presents new peak at 245 nm suggesting the charge transition of n→δ∗ arising from amide groups on nitrogen doped graphene. Graphene@Au shows smaller slop of straight absorption edge between 300–500 nm attributing to the regular sp3 hybridized structure of amide groups (n-π∗ transition) on nitrogen-doped graphene. Graphene@Au also shows the absorption peak of graphene quantum dots indicating plenty of there are still many graphene quantum dots adsorbing on in situ formed graphene, and strong absorption in the visible light region is due to the LSPR effect of Au nanoparticles. The improved absorption of graphene@Au compared with N-GQDs in wavelength larger than 300 nm may result in good photocatalytic performances of catalysts.

In order to achieve overall water splitting, the conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) position of photocatalysts should be more negative and positive than the reduction potential of H2 (0 V vs. RHE) (the reversible hydrogen electrode) and the oxidation potential of O2 (1.23 V vs. RHE) or H2O2 (1.78 V vs. RHE), respectively [7]. Herein, the potential of the conduction band edge (E CB) of graphene@Au was measured by Mott-Schottky (MS) tests (Fig. 4 (b)). E CB = −0.18 V can be determined by the intercept on the x-axis in the MS plots. The ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) spectrum of graphene@Au is applied to determine the valence band energy (E VB), which is shown in Fig. 4 (c). The E VB of graphene@Au is 7.61 eV obtained from E VB = hv - (E cutoff - E onset) in which hv equals to the excitation energy of the He I UPS spectrum (21.22 eV). After converting to electrochemical potential, the E
VB of graphene@Au is 3.17 V vs. the RHE, where 0 V vs. the RHE is equal to −4.44 eV vs. the Evac (vacuum level). Fig. 4 (d) presents the band structure diagram of graphene@Au compared to the potentials of water reduction and oxidation. It is manifested that graphene@Au with appropriate CB and VB positions can theoretically realize the water splitting into H2 and H2O2.

The PL spectra of catalysts reflect the recombination behavior of photogenerated charge carriers, which was shown in Fig. 5 (a). The PL intensity of N-GQDs is much stronger than that of graphene@Au, suggesting the rapid recombination of photogenerated carriers on N-GQDs. However, the PL of the graphene@Au almost completely disappears, indicating the good charge separation efficiency of graphene@Au, arising from the good interface between graphene and Au nanoparticle. The photocurrent of N-GQDs and graphene@Au is tested to further study the photogenerated charges transfer and separation efficiency under full light irradiation. The photocurrent density of graphene@Au is almost 5 fold higher than that of N-GQDs (Fig. 5 (b)). The higher photocurrent of graphene@Au reveals a greater number of photogenerated charges and more effective separation of electron-hole pairs.

Fig. 5 (c) and (d) shows the photocatalytic overall water splitting activity of the catalysts. The photocatalytic activity of samples towards water splitting was carried out under full spectrum illumination without any sacrificial agents or cocatalysts. From Fig. 5 (c) and (d), graphene@Au exhibits a good activity with a H2 evolution rate of 65.6 μmol g−1 h−1 and H2O2 evolution rate of 49.7 μmol g−1 h−1. However, N-GQDs, bare Au and graphene did not show any activity for photocatalytic overall water splitting under the same testing conditions, and graphene@Au also did not exhibit any activity under visible light (Vis) irradiation. As we all know, N-GQDs can be used alone as a photocatalyst [16,22,56] and nitrogen doped graphene is beneficial to the generation of H2O2 [19,64]. Therefore, the results of comparative experiments in Fig. 5 (d) demonstrate that the photogenerated electrons and holes for overall water splitting should come from N-GQDs and Au only provides more hot electrons for H2 generation by LSPR. This may be the reason why the product evolution rate of H2 is slightly higher than that of H2O2 (Fig. 5 (c)). The hot electrons excited by LSPR transfer to the CB of N-GQDs, and then these hot electrons and photogenerated electrons on CB of N-GQDs together reduce H+ to H2, while the photogenerated holes on the VB of N-GQDs transfer to the N-doped graphene to oxide water into H2O2 through two-electron pathway [64]. The benefit of the designed structure of graphene@Au is that the interaction between graphene and Au nanoparticles could be very close, facilitating the fast electrons transfer. The in situ formed nitrogen doped graphene via amide bond plays two important roles: chemically interacting with Au nanoparticle and providing enough active sites for slow water splitting reactions. The excited hot electrons on Au nanoparticles can quickly transfer to the CB of N-GQDs to assist H2 generation, and the photogenerated holes on VB of GQDs can transfer to the N-doped graphene for the oxidation of water to H2O2 as shown in Fig. 6 . Furthermore, to make a comprehensive comparison to other different photocatalysts for the same products, we summarized the splitting efficiency of these catalysts based on references in Table 1 .

In this work, we demonstrated a route for the facile fabrication of graphene@Au photocatalysts with good interfaces for efficient overall water splitting. The prepared graphene@Au exhibits photocatalytic overall water splitting activity with a H2 evolution rate of 65.6 μmol g−1 h−1 and H2O2 evolution rate of 49.7 μmol g−1 h−1. Compared with inert activity of nitrogen doped graphene, the activity of graphene@Au can be ascribed to the charge separation of graphene and LSPR effect of Au which gives the catalyst good response to UV–vis light irradiation. Our research provides an important idea for the bottom-up synthesis of graphene and the design of photocatalysts to simultaneously produce H2 and H2O2.

Junwei Liang: prepared and characterized the catalysts. wrote part of the manuscript. Yuxiang Liu: wrote part of the manuscript. Zhichun Si: designed the whole research plan and initiated this project. Guodan Wei: helped in the characterization of catalysts and modified the manuscript. Duan Weng: helped in the characterization of catalysts and modified the manuscript. Feiyu Kang: helped in the characterization of catalysts and modified the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the National Key Research and Development Program of China for financial support of project 2016YFC0700902-6. Moreover, we would also thank the financial support from Basic Research Project of Shenzhen, China (Grant No. JCYJ20160301150948119).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Scalable water splitting on particulate photocatalyst sheets with a solar-to-hydrogen energy conversion efficiency exceeding 1%

Domen, Scalable water splitting on particulate photocatalyst sheets with a solar-to-hydrogen energy conversion efficiency exceeding 1%, Nat. Mater. 15 (2016) 611-615.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Control strategy on two-/four-electron pathway of water splitting by multidoped carbon based catalysts

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical photolysis of water at a semiconductor electrode

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Fujishima, K. Honda, Electrochemical Photolysis of Water at a Semiconductor Electrode, Nature 238 (1972) 37-38.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Particulate photocatalysts for overall water splitting

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Efficient photocatalytic water splitting through titanium silicalite stabilized CoO nanodots

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots anchored on octahedral CoO as a stable visible-light-responsive composite photocatalyst for overall water splitting

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photocatalytic H2O2 and H2 generation from living chlorella vulgaris and carbon micro particle comodified g-C3N4
